Fern Por-rrrcAl PnecrrcEs C oMMrssroN
-128 J Street r Suire 620 o Sacramenro. CA 95gl.l_2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
January 6,2010
Richard Rushton
8038 Griffith Lane
Sutter, California 95982
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-09-260
Dear Mr. Rushton:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the post- governmental
employment provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "AcI).' pl-ease not" tf,ut there may be
other laws outside the Act that may apply to your situation, including but not limited to Sections
1090 et seq. of the Government Code regarding contracts and Public Contract Coder Section
IO4ll. The Commission's advice is limited to matters arising under the Act. (Regulation
18329(bX8Xd).) we urge you to check with the state Attorney General's office or your personal
counsel to determine if there are other laws that may be applicable in light of the facts you
present.
QUESTTONS
1. 
May you, as a retired designated employee of the Califomia Highway patrol
("CHI"'; whose work involved development of bid specifications for maintenice and
procurements related to aviation, bid on behalf of your business on contracts with CHp or any
other state agency that puts out contracts for competitive bid?
2. 
May you work for a current vendor of CFIP who has won a competitive bid?
3. 
May you purchase a Federal Aviation Administration license for a specific
product installation and then enter into an agreement with a CHp vendor pursuant to which the
vendor would use that "one-time" license to install the product on a CHp aircraft?
'ThePoliticalRefbrmActiscontainedinGovernmentCodesectionsSl000throughgl0l4. 
All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Foi, politi.ul practices
Commission are contained in Sections I8l l0 through l8gg7 of Title 2 of ttri California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
qlB


File No. A-09-260
Page No. 2
4- 
If the answer to any of the foregoing questions is "no," may you perform the
contemplated work or actions as an employee of youi wife's solely orrn.d business?
CONCLUSIONS
1' You are not barred by the'permanent ban" from bidding on contracts with
cHP or other state agencies because your work at cHP consisted oiarartinjbio specifications
applicable to all biddet. T9 not to any identifiable specific party. The permanent ban only
covers proceedings that affect the rights or claims ofspecific parties. Tire one-year ban does not
prohibit you from appearing or communicating with CHP forihe purpose of bidding on these
contracts because you will be doing so solely to represent your personal interest. Tf,e one-year
ban applies when a former employee is being compensat"i fo, hi, o, t "r 
upp"urunce on behalf of
another person.
2' You may perform work for a current CHP maintenance vendor so long as you
are not identified with the vendor's efforts to influence CHp, as discussed below.
3' You may acquire an FAA license for a specific product installation and
contract with a cHP vendor to use that license to initall the product on a CHp aircraft so long as
your involvement with the vendor is limited to providing the license and you are not identified
with the vendor's efforts to influence CHP's award of tlie contract, as discussed below.
4' It makes no difference whether you bid on a CFIP contract or perform work
for a cHP vendor as an owner of your business oras an employee of a business owned by your
spouse' The post-gov€mmental employment provisions orihe ect bar ro.*"igorernment
employees (individuals) from engaging in certain conduct and do not apply to flusinesses.
FACTS
You retired from CHP on November 13, 2009 as a uniformed sergeant assigned to the
office of Air operations with the title of Aviation Maintenance supervisir. your unit developed
bid specifications for maintenance and procurement related to aviation. All specifications
drafted by your unit were_ reviewed by you. After these specifications were reviewed by you,
your unit commander and the division commander, they were submitted to the cHp contracts
unit and'/or CHP Purchasing Services unit which put contracts or procurements out to bid. your
unit was not involved with the selection of vendois.
You are planning o_n starting your own aviation consulting business. you would like
your company to bid on CHP or other state agency contracts. You would also like to work for a
cument CHP maintenance vendor who won a competitive bid. tn addition, you ask whether you
m1Y Purchase a Supplemental Type Certificate, which is a Federal Aviation Administration
("FAA") license that signifies FAA's approval of a specific product installation. on an aircraft.
once you acquire the license, you intend to enter into an agreement with a cHp vendor to install
the product' The vendor would pay your company for a orie-time license to install the product on


File No. A-09-260
Page No. 3
a CHP aircraft. You are considering having your wife, who retired from CHp three years ago,
own the business, in which case you would be an employee.
In our telephone conversation of December 15, 2009, you confirmed that you were a
designated employee in the CHP conflict-of-interest code and filed Statements of Economic
lnterest.
ANALYSIS
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-govemmental
employment provisions under the Act, colloquially known as the'ievotving door,, prohibitions.
We look to these provisions to answer your questions.
I. 
Permanent Ban: ,.Switching Sides',
The first restriction is the "permanent ban" prohibiting a former state administrative
official from "switching sides" and participating, for compensation, in any specific proceeding
involving the State of California, or assisting otlers in the proceeding, if the proceeding is one in
which the former administrative official partlcipated while employ"Juy the state (See Sections
87401-87402 and Regulation 18741.1.) t'Stut" udrninistrative official" is defined as ..every
member, officer, employee or consultant of a state administrative agency who, as part of his or
her official responsibility, engages in any judicial, quasi-judicial or"other proceeoing in other
than a purely clerical, secretarial or miniiterial capatity.,; lsection g7400fu).)
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other
proceeding in which the official participated.whili employed. ;'Judi"iul, quasi-judicial or other
proceeding" means any proceeding, application, .rqu"rt for a ruling or other determination,
contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, iccusation, arrest or other particular matter
involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative agencf. lsection
87a00(c)') Additionally, * official is considered to have "participated" In a iroceeding if he or
she took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantialiy through oecisioir, approval,
disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering advice on isubstantiaibasis,
investigation' or use of confidential information . . .." fsection g7a00(d)); or, if th, proceeding
was under his or her "supervisory authority." (section 87401, Regulation lg74l.l (aX4).)
we focus our analysis on determining whether any of the actions in which you now wish
to engage involve a "proceeding" in which you participatld while employed ui crrp.
(Regulation I 8741 . I (aX4).) You state that one of youi primary duties at cHp was to review all
bid specifications developed by your unit for mainienance and procurement related to aviation
before the specifications were submitted to your unit commander, the division cotrmander and,
finally, to the units that put contracts or procurements out to bid. Contracts are specifically
included in the definition of 'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding.', (section g7a00(c).)


File No. A-09-260
Page No.4
However, the permanent ban only covers proceedings, including applications for a
contract, that affect the rights or claims of specific parties. It does ,ot u'ppiy to those that involve
the making of rules or policies of general applicabiiit y . (Beate Advice i.ttlr; No. A-00- 146.)
Fo-r example, we previously advised that a itut. ud*irristrative official who, as part of the
official's state emploY.menl, developed a system for setting foster care rates'applicable to all
group homes was not involved in a'Judicial, quasi-judicia-l or other proceeding,, because the
system did not involve any one specific group home. lBersingerAdvice Letter; A-g2-209.) We
have also advised that the formulation oia rate design poli.y io. the public Utilities Commission
was not a 'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other p-roceeding"-because the policy had an industry-wide
application, and did 19t lo:rl on any rp""ifi" vtility. (FongAdvice Letter; 4-88-024.) In the
Metz Advice Letter, No. I-06-147, we concluded thrt ; retiied captain of theDepartment of
corrections and Rehabilitation who developed a draft plan for deiivery of community treatment
services to parolees was not subject to the permanent ban because the plan merety set forth
options for how the services would be delivered and the plan did not involve the rights or claims
of identifiable specific parties. Your participation in the iro".r. leading up to the granting of a
contract consisted of drafting or approving drafrc of bid specifications. It ls our understanding
that these specifications were appiicable to all bidders on'ury resulting contract and that, at this
stage of a contract's life cycle, there was no identifiable specific party in mind.
Therefore, we conclude that your work at CHP did not involve participation in a
proceeding that affected the rights of specific parties, but instead, involved the making of
specifications of general applicability. Accordingly, the actions you are proporirrg in your four
questions are not prohibited by the life_time ban.
II. 
The One-Year Banl ,.Revolving Door,,
The second restriction is the "one-year ban," which prohibits a state employee, for one
year after leaving state service, and for compensation, from communicating with his or her
folmer agency for the purpose of influencing certain administrative or tegiltative action or
influencing certainproceedings. (See sectio-n 87406;Regulation 1g746.i.) In contrast to the
permanent ban, which only applies to'Judicial or quasi-jirdicial" pro"e"oirg", the one-year ban
applies to any "appearance or communication" foi nmonths from the datJof your separation
from employment with cI{P. while in effect, the one-year ban prohiuits anv ro.-al or informal
appearance or oral or written communication with youi fo.-e, agency for the principal purpose
of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifyln_g, opposing, delaying, or advancing the action or
proceeding. (Regulations 1g746.1(b) and Biq6Z)'
An appearance or communication includes, but is not limited to, conversing by telephone
or in person, correspdnding in writing or by electronic transmission, attending a meeting, and
delivering or sending any communicition ifor the purpose of influencing aoriinistrative or
legislative action, or any discretionary action to influence any action or proceeding involving the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, litense, gra;, o, "on,ro",, or the sale
or purchase of goods or properry." (Regulations 1g746.1(b)(5xc) ind rg7a6.z(a).)


File No. A-09-260
Page No. 5
1.
The one-year ban applies when a former employee or official is being compensated for
his or her appearances or communications before his oi her former agency i u"noy oy another
person. (Section 87406; Regulation 18746.1(b)(3).) You indicate ttr-at you would rlpiesent your
personally owned company when bidding on these contracts. Regulation 18746.1(bX4) provides
that an "appearance or communication made by a public official iolely to represent his or her
personal interest, as defined in Regulation 18702.4(bXl), is not prohibited or limited.,'
(Emphasis added). Regulation ls7o2.4(b)(1) provides that a puuti" official is not attempting to
use his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision of an agency if the official
"appears in the same manner as any other member of the general public befire an agency in the
course of its prescribed govemmental function solely to ripreseni himsey or herself on a matter
whtch is related to his or her personal interesfs." (Emphasis added.) Tlie definition of ..personal
interests" includes a business entity wholly owned by tire official or members of the official,s
immediate family. (Regulationl87o2.4(b)(1)(B).) when you appear before rhe cHp to bid on a
contract on behalf of a company that either you or you. .poure own, you will be representing
your personal business interest. When T*rlg such bids, you will neither be representing
another person nor receiving compensation from anoth", pr.ron as proscribed by ,n" orrslyear
ban. Therefore, the one-year ban does nol prohibit you, ytu, "o-pany or your wife,s company
from bidding on contracts offered by cHp or any oiher siate agency.
2.
Section 87406 does not prevent you from advising or assisting others, either as an
employee or as an independent consultant, in their pursuil of state coitracts. However, in doing
so, you are prohibited from making any formal or informal appearance, or making any oral or
written communication, before your former employer or other state agencies if the appearance or
communication is made for the,purpose of influeniing administratlvJor legislative u"iiorr, o,
influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, iwarding or revocation
of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the ,al" or purchase of goods o. prop.rty. (Regulation
fi7a6.2(a).)
The Commissionlas previously advised that a former agency official could draft
proposals on a client's behalf to be submitted to the agency ,, tirg is the former employee was
not identified in connection with the client's efforts ti "injlrrrnce administrative action.,,
(Harrison Advice Letter, No. 4-92-289); MillerAdvice Letter, No. I-93-09g; and Kingma-
Rymek Advice Letter. No. A-95-141.) Similarly, a former agency official may use his or her
expertise to advise clients on the procedural requirements, plans,tr policies .i rh. "E;i;;'
former agency so long as the employee is not iientified wltth the ,*ploy"r', efforts to influence
the agency. (Perry Advice Letter, No. 4-94-004.) therefore, your proposed work for a CHp
vendor is not prohibited by the one-year ban so long as you are not identified with the vendor,s
efforts to influence CHP.


File No. A-09-260
Page No. 6
3
As stated above, Section 87406does not prevent you from advising or assisting others,
either as an employ-T oI as an independent contractor, in their pursuit of siate contracts. you
are, however, prohibited from making any formal or informal appearance or from making any
oral or written communication, before y^our former employer oiother state agencies if the
appearance or corlmunication is made for the purpose of influencing any u"ion or proceeding
involving the issuance, amendment, awarding or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or
contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property.
If your involvement with the vendor's contract with CHp is limited to providing the
necessary FAA license, there would be no violation of the one-year ban. However, if you assist
the vendor in drafting his-or her proposal !o cHP, you could be violating the one-year ban unless
you are not tdentffied with the vendor's efforts to influence CFIp in awalding the contract.
4. Ownership of the Business bv your Spouse
our analysis does not change if you perform the work you have described as an employee
of a business owned by your spouse. The prohibition of section g7406(dx1) applies to an
"employee." only individuals can be "-pioy""r. Therefore, ]ou, and not the business, are
subject to the provisions of the one-year bun. 1s"" also Section 87406(e), referring to an
individual.)
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
aru^u')a7-
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division
vJjgl

legislative action, or any discretionary action to influence any action or proceeding involving the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, litense, gra;, o, "on,ro",, or the sale
or purchase of goods or properry." (Regulations 1g746.1(b)(5xc) ind rg7a6.z(a).)
1.
The one-year ban applies when a former employee or official is being compensated for
his or her appearances or communications before his oi her former agency i u"noy oy another
person. (Section 87406; Regulation 18746.1(b)(3).) You indicate ttr-at you would rlpiesent your
personally owned company when bidding on these contracts. Regulation 18746.1(bX4) provides
that an "appearance or communication made by a public official iolely to represent his or her
personal interest, as defined in Regulation 18702.4(bXl), is not prohibited or limited.,'
(Emphasis added). Regulation ls7o2.4(b)(1) provides that a puuti" official is not attempting to
use his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision of an agency if the official
"appears in the same manner as any other member of the general public befire an agency in the
course of its prescribed govemmental function solely to ripreseni himsey or herself on a matter
whtch is related to his or her personal interesfs." (Emphasis added.) Tlie definition of ..personal
interests" includes a business entity wholly owned by tire official or members of the official,s
immediate family. (Regulationl87o2.4(b)(1)(B).) when you appear before rhe cHp to bid on a
contract on behalf of a company that either you or you. .poure own, you will be representing
your personal business interest. When T*rlg such bids, you will neither be representing
another person nor receiving compensation from anoth", pr.ron as proscribed by ,n" orrslyear
ban. Therefore, the one-year ban does nol prohibit you, ytu, "o-pany or your wife,s company
from bidding on contracts offered by cHp or any oiher siate agency.
2.
Section 87406 does not prevent you from advising or assisting others, either as an
employee or as an independent consultant, in their pursuil of state coitracts. However, in doing
so, you are prohibited from making any formal or informal appearance, or making any oral or
written communication, before your former employer or other state agencies if the appearance or
communication is made for the,purpose of influeniing administratlvJor legislative u"iiorr, o,
influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, iwarding or revocation
of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the ,al" or purchase of goods o. prop.rty. (Regulation
fi7a6.2(a).)
The Commissionlas previously advised that a former agency official could draft
proposals on a client's behalf to be submitted to the agency ,, tirg is the former employee was
not identified in connection with the client's efforts ti "injlrrrnce administrative action.,,
(Harrison Advice Letter, No. 4-92-289); MillerAdvice Letter, No. I-93-09g; and Kingma-
Rymek Advice Letter. No. A-95-141.) Similarly, a former agency official may use his or her
expertise to advise clients on the procedural requirements, plans,tr policies .i rh. "E;i;;'
former agency so long as the employee is not iientified wltth the ,*ploy"r', efforts to influence
the agency. (Perry Advice Letter, No. 4-94-004.) therefore, your proposed work for a CHp
vendor is not prohibited by the one-year ban so long as you are not identified with the vendor,s
efforts to influence CHP.
3
As stated above, Section 87406does not prevent you from advising or assisting others,
either as an employ-T oI as an independent contractor, in their pursuit of siate contracts. you
are, however, prohibited from making any formal or informal appearance or from making any
oral or written communication, before y^our former employer oiother state agencies if the
appearance or corlmunication is made for the purpose of influencing any u"ion or proceeding
involving the issuance, amendment, awarding or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or
contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property.
If your involvement with the vendor's contract with CHp is limited to providing the
necessary FAA license, there would be no violation of the one-year ban. However, if you assist
the vendor in drafting his-or her proposal !o cHP, you could be violating the one-year ban unless
you are not tdentffied with the vendor's efforts to influence CFIp in awalding the contract.
4. Ownership of the Business bv your Spouse
our analysis does not change if you perform the work you have described as an employee
of a business owned by your spouse. The prohibition of section g7406(dx1) applies to an
"employee." only individuals can be "-pioy""r. Therefore, ]ou, and not the business, are
subject to the provisions of the one-year bun. 1s"" also Section 87406(e), referring to an
individual.)

CONCLUSION: 1' You are not barred by the'permanent ban" from bidding on contracts with
cHP or other state agencies because your work at cHP consisted oiarartinjbio specifications
applicable to all biddet. T9 not to any identifiable specific party. The permanent ban only
covers proceedings that affect the rights or claims ofspecific parties. Tire one-year ban does not
prohibit you from appearing or communicating with CHP forihe purpose of bidding on these
contracts because you will be doing so solely to represent your personal interest. Tf,e one-year
ban applies when a former employee is being compensat"i fo, hi, o, t "r 
upp"urunce on behalf of
another person.
2' You may perform work for a current CHP maintenance vendor so long as you
are not identified with the vendor's efforts to influence CHp, as discussed below.
3' You may acquire an FAA license for a specific product installation and
contract with a cHP vendor to use that license to initall the product on a CHp aircraft so long as
your involvement with the vendor is limited to providing the license and you are not identified
with the vendor's efforts to influence CHP's award of tlie contract, as discussed below.
4' It makes no difference whether you bid on a CFIP contract or perform work
for a cHP vendor as an owner of your business oras an employee of a business owned by your
spouse' The post-gov€mmental employment provisions orihe ect bar ro.*"igorernment
employees (individuals) from engaging in certain conduct and do not apply to flusinesses.
